events.title: Korea Divided at the 38th Parallel
events.detail: Following Japan's August 1945 surrender ending World War II, the US and Soviet Union divided the Korean Peninsula at the 38th parallel for the purposes of accepting Japanese surrender. The US occupied the south; the Soviet Union occupied the north. This administrative division, intended as temporary, would become permanent. Korean independence leaders who had fought Japanese colonization since 1910 were sidelined as superpower rivalry took precedence.

sources.name: Bruce Cumings, 'The Origins of the Korean War' (Princeton, 1981)

events.title: Kim Il-sung Installed as Soviet-Backed Leader
events.detail: Kim Il-sung, a 33-year-old Korean guerrilla fighter who had served in Soviet forces, was installed by the Soviet Union as leader of the Soviet Military Administration's client state in northern Korea. Stalin preferred Kim over older Korean communists. Kim had spent the war in the Soviet Union and commanded the Soviet-trained 1st Battalion of the 88th International Brigade.

sources.name: Andrei Lankov, 'From Stalin to Kim Il Sung' (Rutgers, 2002)

events.title: DPRK Proclaimed — Kim Il-sung Named Premier
events.detail: The Democratic People's Republic of Korea was formally proclaimed on September 9, 1948, with Kim Il-sung as Premier. Three weeks earlier, on August 15, Syngman Rhee's Republic of Korea had been proclaimed in the south. Both regimes claimed sovereignty over the entire Korean Peninsula. The Soviet Union withdrew its troops from the north in late 1948; the US withdrew from the south in June 1949.

sources.name: United Nations Commission on Korea records

events.title: Kim Il-sung Seeks Stalin's Approval for Invasion
events.detail: Kim Il-sung sent multiple requests to Stalin in 1949 seeking approval and military support for a military campaign to reunify Korea under DPRK control. Stalin initially refused, fearing US intervention. After the Soviet Union's first nuclear test (August 1949) and the victory of Mao's communist forces in China (October 1949), Stalin changed his assessment. By January 1950 Stalin had conditionally approved the plan.

sources.name: Kathryn Weathersby, 'Soviet Aims in Korea and the Origins of the Korean War' (CWIHP, 1993)

events.title: North Korea Invades South Korea — Korean War Begins
events.detail: At 4:00 AM on June 25, 1950, the Korean People's Army (KPA) crossed the 38th parallel with 75,000 troops supported by Soviet-supplied T-34 tanks. The ill-prepared ROK Army collapsed within days; Seoul fell on June 28. The UN Security Council — with the Soviet Union absent in protest over Taiwan's seat — authorized a US-led military response. President Truman committed US forces on June 27. The KPA drove UN/ROK forces to a small perimeter around Pusan by August.

sources.name: US Department of Defense Korean War records

events.title: MacArthur's Inchon Landing — UN Forces Counterattack
events.detail: Gen. Douglas MacArthur's daring amphibious assault at Inchon on September 15, 1950 turned the tide of the war. Despite tidal and logistical challenges dismissed as near-impossible by the US Navy, the landing succeeded in cutting KPA supply lines. UN forces liberated Seoul on September 28 and crossed the 38th parallel by October 1, advancing toward the Chinese border at the Yalu River.

sources.name: US Army Center of Military History — Korean War

events.title: China Enters the Korean War — 300,000 Troops Cross the Yalu
events.detail: As UN forces approached the Chinese border, the People's Liberation Army crossed the Yalu River in late October 1950 with approximately 300,000 troops. MacArthur had dismissed intelligence warnings. Chinese forces encircled and destroyed UN units in the Chosin Reservoir campaign and drove UN forces back south of the 38th parallel by January 1951. MacArthur publicly demanded authority to attack China, leading Truman to relieve him in April 1951.

sources.name: US Department of Defense Korean War records

events.title: Battle of Chosin Reservoir — 'Frozen Chosin'
events.detail: November 27–December 13, 1950: 30,000 UN forces (primarily US Marines and Army, plus South Korean and British units) were encircled by 120,000 Chinese troops in temperatures of –35°C. After 17 days of fighting withdrawal to Hungnam, approximately 4,500 US personnel were killed or missing. Chinese forces suffered catastrophic losses estimated at 25,000+ dead. The 105,000 evacuees at Hungnam included 91,000 North Korean civilian refugees.

sources.name: US Marines Corps History Division — Battle of Chosin Reservoir

events.title: Armistice Negotiations Begin — Two-Year Stalemate
events.detail: Armistice talks began at Kaesong on July 10, 1951 as the front stabilized near the 38th parallel after both sides suffered enormous casualties. Negotiations would drag on for two years, primarily over the issue of prisoner repatriation — North Korea and China demanded all POWs be returned, while the US insisted on voluntary repatriation. Fierce fighting continued during negotiations, including the bloody battles of Heartbreak Ridge and Pork Chop Hill.

sources.name: US Department of State Foreign Relations of the United States (FRUS) 1951

events.title: Korean Armistice Signed — War Suspended, Not Ended
events.detail: The Korean Armistice Agreement was signed at Panmunjom on July 27, 1953 by the KPA/PVA and UN Command — but NOT by South Korea, whose president Rhee had refused to sign. The armistice established the Military Demarcation Line close to the 38th parallel and a 4-km wide Demilitarized Zone (DMZ). No peace treaty was ever signed; technically the Korean War has never officially ended. The armistice has held for over 70 years despite periodic crises.

sources.name: Korean Armistice Agreement (1953) — UN Treaty Collection

events.title: Kim Il-sung Purges Soviet and Chinese Factions
events.detail: At the 3rd WPK Congress in April 1956, Kim Il-sung moved against Soviet-aligned and Chinese-aligned factions within the party. The so-called 'August Faction Incident' saw Kim resist an internal attempt to criticize him following Khrushchev's de-Stalinization speech. By 1958, Kim had purged hundreds of party members and military officers, establishing personal control over all state institutions — laying the groundwork for the world's most totalitarian state.

sources.name: Andrei Lankov, 'Crisis in North Korea: The Failure of De-Stalinization' (Hawaii, 2005)

events.title: USS Pueblo Intelligence Ship Seized
events.detail: North Korean naval vessels seized the US Navy intelligence ship USS Pueblo (83 crew) in international waters off Wonsan on January 23, 1968 — one day after the Tet Offensive in Vietnam. The crew was held and tortured for 11 months. The US was forced to sign a statement (later repudiated) admitting espionage to secure the crew's release in December 1968. The Pueblo remains in Pyongyang's Taedong River as a museum and propaganda display.

sources.name: US Navy Historical Center — USS Pueblo Incident

events.title: Juche Ideology Enshrined in DPRK Constitution
events.detail: The 1972 DPRK constitution formally replaced Marxism-Leninism with Juche (self-reliance) as the state's guiding ideology. Kim Il-sung was named President; a separate Supreme People's Assembly and State Administration Council were established. Juche emphasized political independence, economic self-reliance, and national defense self-sufficiency — partially rationalizing North Korea's growing isolation from both the Soviet Union and China.

events.title: Rangoon Bombing — Failed Assassination of South Korean President
events.detail: On October 9, 1983, DPRK agents detonated a bomb at the National Martyrs' Mausoleum in Rangoon, Myanmar, targeting South Korean President Chun Doo-hwan. Chun survived (arriving late) but 21 people were killed, including 4 cabinet ministers and 17 other South Korean officials. Two DPRK agents were captured; one confessed to being a Reconnaissance General Bureau operative. Burma severed diplomatic relations with DPRK.

events.title: KAL Flight 858 Bombing — 115 Killed
events.detail: Korean Air Flight 858 was destroyed by a bomb planted by DPRK agents on November 29, 1987, killing all 115 people on board over the Andaman Sea. The agents were two DPRK Reconnaissance Bureau operatives posing as Japanese tourists who left the plane in Abu Dhabi. Agent Kim Hyun-hee was captured in Bahrain, confessed, and was later pardoned by South Korea after cooperating. The bombing aimed to disrupt the 1988 Seoul Olympics.

sources.name: Republic of Korea Ministry of Foreign Affairs

events.title: North and South Korea Simultaneously Join the United Nations
events.detail: On September 17, 1991, both the Democratic People's Republic of Korea and the Republic of Korea were admitted to the United Nations simultaneously. DPRK had long opposed separate UN membership as it implied acceptance of permanent division. The Soviet Union's collapse in 1991 removed DPRK's UN Security Council veto protection, forcing the change. The same year, North and South signed the Agreement on Reconciliation, Nonaggression, and Exchanges and Cooperation.

events.title: First Nuclear Crisis — DPRK Withdraws from NPT
events.detail: North Korea announced withdrawal from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) on March 12, 1993, after IAEA inspectors demanded access to undeclared nuclear waste sites at Yongbyon. It was the first time any country had tried to withdraw from the NPT. Negotiations with the Clinton administration led DPRK to suspend withdrawal in June 1993. The crisis set the template for decades of DPRK nuclear brinkmanship: threaten, negotiate, extract concessions, repeat.

media.source: Wikipedia / Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons

events.title: Kim Il-sung Dies — Kim Jong-il Inherits Power
events.detail: Kim Il-sung died of a heart attack on July 8, 1994, at age 82, in the midst of nuclear negotiations with the Clinton administration. His death plunged the country into a three-year public mourning period. Kim Jong-il, who had been officially designated heir since 1974, assumed all power but did not take the title of President — declaring Kim Il-sung the 'eternal President.' Negotiations nonetheless led to the October 1994 Agreed Framework, a landmark deal freezing North Korea's plutonium program.

sources.name: US State Department — Agreed Framework records

media.source: Wikipedia / Death and state funeral of Kim Il Sung

events.title: US-DPRK Agreed Framework — Nuclear Freeze Deal
events.detail: On October 21, 1994, the United States and DPRK signed the Agreed Framework: North Korea agreed to freeze and eventually dismantle its plutonium program in exchange for two light-water reactors (built by a US-led consortium KEDO), 500,000 tons of heavy fuel oil annually, normalization of relations, and security assurances. The framework held until 2002 but was undermined by mutual non-compliance accusations and the revelation of a secret DPRK uranium enrichment program.

sources.name: Agreed Framework between USA and DPRK (1994) — US State Department

era: Kim Jong-il: Famine, Missiles & Nuclear Emergence (1994–2011)

events.title: Arduous March Famine — 600,000–1M Estimated Deaths
events.detail: The collapse of Soviet aid, catastrophic floods in 1995–1996, and the regime's prioritization of military spending over food distribution caused a massive famine. An estimated 600,000–1 million people died of starvation and related disease, with some estimates reaching 3.5 million. Kim Jong-il named the period the 'Arduous March.' The regime appealed to the UN and NGOs for food aid but simultaneously refused to allow adequate humanitarian monitoring. The famine permanently traumatized North Korean society.

sources.name: WFP / UNICEF / UNFPA DPRK food assessment 1998
Stephan Haggard & Marcus Noland, 'Famine in North Korea' (Columbia, 2007)

events.title: First Inter-Korean Summit — Kim Dae-jung & Kim Jong-il
events.detail: South Korean President Kim Dae-jung travelled to Pyongyang on June 13–15, 2000 for the first-ever summit between the two Korean leaders. The June 15 Joint Declaration pledged to work toward reunification and expand cooperation. The meeting earned Kim Dae-jung the Nobel Peace Prize. It inaugurated the Sunshine Policy era: South Korean investment flowed north, separated families were reunited, and the Kaesong Industrial Complex was established. Kim Jong-il received $500M in secret funds to agree to the summit.

events.title: Bush 'Axis of Evil' Speech — New Nuclear Crisis Begins
events.detail: President George W. Bush's January 2002 State of the Union address named North Korea, Iran, and Iraq as an 'Axis of Evil.' The speech alarmed Pyongyang, which viewed it as threatening regime change. In October 2002, US envoy James Kelly confronted DPRK officials with evidence of a secret uranium enrichment program, which Assistant Secretary Kang Seok-ju reportedly acknowledged. The Agreed Framework collapsed; KEDO suspended fuel shipments in November 2002, and DPRK declared itself no longer bound by the framework.

sources.name: White House State of the Union Address (Jan 29, 2002)
US State Department — Kelly October 2002 mission report

events.title: DPRK Withdraws from NPT (Second Time)
events.detail: On January 10, 2003, North Korea announced its formal withdrawal from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) — the first country to successfully complete withdrawal. DPRK expelled IAEA inspectors from Yongbyon and removed IAEA monitoring seals from its plutonium reprocessing facility. Six-Party Talks (US, China, Russia, Japan, South and North Korea) began in August 2003 but failed to produce a lasting agreement. IAEA lost all monitoring access to DPRK's nuclear program.

sources.name: IAEA Board of Governors — Director General report (Feb 2003)

media.source: Wikipedia / Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons

events.title: First North Korean Nuclear Test at Punggye-ri
events.detail: On October 9, 2006, North Korea conducted its first underground nuclear test at the Punggye-ri site in North Hamgyong Province. The CTBTO detected a seismic event of magnitude 4.1; US intelligence estimated a yield of less than 1 kiloton — suggesting a partial fizzle, though DPRK claimed full success. The UN Security Council unanimously passed Resolution 1718 imposing the first comprehensive sanctions on DPRK's nuclear and missile programs.

events.title: Second Nuclear Test — Yield Increases to ~6 kt
events.detail: DPRK conducted its second nuclear test on May 25, 2009, generating a seismic event of magnitude 4.7 and an estimated yield of 2–6 kilotons. The test came shortly after the UN condemned DPRK's April 2009 satellite launch (Kwangmyongsong-2). China publicly condemned the test, a notable diplomatic shift. UN Security Council passed Resolution 1874, tightening sanctions and authorizing cargo inspections of suspected DPRK ships.

events.title: ROKS Cheonan Sunk — 46 South Korean Sailors Killed
events.detail: South Korean corvette ROKS Cheonan split in two and sank in the Yellow Sea on March 26, 2010, killing 46 sailors. A Multinational Joint Investigative Group (South Korea, US, UK, Australia, Sweden) concluded a North Korean CHT-02D torpedo caused the explosion. DPRK denied involvement. South Korea invoked the 5.24 measures, suspending all inter-Korean trade. UNSC issued a statement of condemnation without naming DPRK, reflecting Chinese and Russian opposition.

sources.name: Multinational Joint Investigation Group final report (2010)
UN Security Council Presidential Statement S/PRST/2010/13

events.confidenceReason: DPRK denial and Russia's inconclusive investigation prevent full verification

events.title: Yeonpyeong Island Artillery Attack — First Attack on South Korean Civilians Since 1953
events.detail: On November 23, 2010, the KPA fired approximately 170 artillery shells at the South Korean-controlled island of Yeonpyeong in the Yellow Sea, killing 2 marines and 2 civilians and injuring 19 others. This was the first North Korean attack on a civilian area in South Korea since the 1953 armistice. The attack occurred near the disputed Northern Limit Line (NLL) during South Korean military exercises. South Korea returned fire with K-9 howitzers. The US deployed the USS George Washington carrier to the Yellow Sea.

events.title: Kim Jong-il Dies — Kim Jong-un Succeeds
events.detail: Kim Jong-il died of a heart attack on December 17, 2011 while travelling by train, aged 69. State media announced his death on December 19. His son Kim Jong-un, who had only been publicly introduced to the world in 2010, took power. Despite Western speculation that the regime might collapse under an untested 28-year-old leader, Kim Jong-un rapidly consolidated control and conducted his first nuclear test within 14 months of taking power.

events.title: Third Nuclear Test — Hiroshima-Scale Yield
events.detail: North Korea conducted its third nuclear test on February 12, 2013, producing a seismic event of magnitude 5.1 and an estimated yield of 6–16 kilotons — comparable to the Hiroshima bomb. DPRK claimed the device was 'miniaturized and lighter,' implying a deliverable warhead. China condemned the test in unusually strong terms. UN Security Council passed Resolution 2094 with the strongest sanctions to date.

media.source: Wikipedia / Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki

events.title: Jang Song-thaek Executed for 'Treason'
events.detail: Kim Jong-un's uncle and political regent, Jang Song-thaek (husband of Kim Jong-un's aunt Kim Kyong-hui), was publicly arrested at a WPK Politburo meeting on December 8, 2013 and executed on December 12. He was charged with 'anti-party, counter-revolutionary factional acts' and corruption. The execution shocked the world and signalled Kim Jong-un's ruthless consolidation of absolute power. Multiple senior officials allegedly executed in a purge.

events.title: UN Commission of Inquiry: DPRK Guilty of Crimes Against Humanity
events.detail: A three-member UN Commission of Inquiry chaired by Australian Justice Michael Kirby released its report on February 17, 2014, concluding that North Korea had committed crimes against humanity — including extermination, murder, enslavement, torture, rape, forced abortion, and persecution — with the 'gravity, scale and nature' comparable to Nazi Germany. The COI recommended referral to the International Criminal Court. China and Russia vetoed the UNSC resolution to refer DPRK to the ICC.

sources.name: UN Commission of Inquiry on DPRK Human Rights — Final Report (A/HRC/25/63), Feb 2014

media.source: Wikipedia / Report of the Commission of Inquiry on Human Rights in the Democratic People's Republic of Korea

events.title: Fourth Nuclear Test — DPRK Claims Hydrogen Bomb
events.detail: DPRK conducted its fourth nuclear test on January 6, 2016, claiming to have detonated a hydrogen bomb. The seismic event of magnitude 5.1 and estimated yield of 6–9 kt were inconsistent with a thermonuclear device, leading the US, South Korea, Japan, and China to dismiss the hydrogen bomb claim as exaggerated. UN Security Council passed Resolution 2270 — the most comprehensive sanctions to date — banning coal, iron, and other commodity exports.

events.title: Kim Jong-nam Killed with VX Nerve Agent in Malaysia
events.detail: Kim Jong-un's exiled half-brother Kim Jong-nam was assassinated at Kuala Lumpur International Airport on February 13, 2017, using VX nerve agent applied by two women who believed they were participating in a prank TV show. Four DPRK suspects identified by police fled to Pyongyang. Malaysia expelled the DPRK ambassador. US Treasury sanctioned North Korean entities for the assassination using a chemical weapon of mass destruction.

events.title: Hwasong-14 ICBM Test — First Confirmed Intercontinental Missile
events.detail: DPRK tested the Hwasong-14 ICBM for the first time on July 4, 2017 — the US Independence Day — from Panghyon air base. The missile reached approximately 2,802 km altitude on a lofted trajectory and flew ~933 km horizontally. US intelligence confirmed ICBM range. A second test on July 28 demonstrated longer range. Kim Jong-un personally supervised both tests. The tests crossed a long-feared capability threshold.

events.title: Sixth Nuclear Test — Largest Ever, Hydrogen Bomb Claim
events.detail: On September 3, 2017, DPRK conducted its sixth and largest nuclear test, producing a seismic event of magnitude 6.3 and a yield estimated at 100–250 kilotons by US and South Korean intelligence. DPRK declared it had tested a 'two-stage thermonuclear weapon' for an ICBM. The explosion was 10× larger than the fifth test. UN Security Council passed Resolution 2375 banning natural gas exports and capping oil imports. The test tunnel collapse destabilized Mantap Mountain at Punggye-ri.

events.title: Trump-Kim Singapore Summit — Historic First Meeting
events.detail: US President Donald Trump and North Korean leader Kim Jong-un held the first-ever summit between a sitting US president and DPRK leader on June 12, 2018 in Singapore. They signed the 'Singapore Declaration' pledging to work toward 'complete denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula' and establish new relations. Trump announced suspension of US-South Korea military exercises. The summit produced no specific denuclearization roadmap, leading critics to call it a diplomatic legitimization of Kim Jong-un.

media.source: Wikipedia / 2018 North Korea%E2%80%93United States Singapore Summit

events.title: Three Moon-Kim Inter-Korean Summits — Panmunjom Declaration
events.detail: South Korean President Moon Jae-in and Kim Jong-un held three summits in 2018: April 27 at Panmunjom (historic handshake across the military demarcation line), May 26 in a surprise second meeting, and September 18-20 in Pyongyang. The Panmunjom Declaration pledged denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula, reducing military tensions, and family reunions. Moon walked into North Korean territory. By late 2019 all engagement had frozen following the Hanoi summit collapse.

events.title: Hanoi Summit Collapses — No Deal
events.detail: The second Trump-Kim summit, held in Hanoi, Vietnam on February 27-28, 2019, ended abruptly without any agreement. Trump and Kim walked away without signing a joint statement. Trump stated DPRK demanded complete sanctions removal in exchange for only partial Yongbyon dismantlement. DPRK Foreign Minister Ri Yong-ho disputed this account, saying only five of eleven sanctions resolutions were sought in exchange for Yongbyon. No subsequent leader-level talks have occurred as of 2026.

sources.name: DPRK Foreign Ministry statement — Ri Yong-ho press conference (Mar 1, 2019)

events.title: DPRK Seals Borders — Total Isolation Due to COVID-19
events.detail: North Korea became one of the first countries to close its borders when Kim Jong-un shut all land, sea, and air access on January 22, 2020, citing COVID-19 risks. The closure halted all trade with China — 92% of DPRK's commerce — causing severe shortages. Diplomatic personnel were stranded for months. DPRK denied having any COVID cases until May 2022. The border closure was not fully lifted until 2023, having caused serious economic deterioration and further food insecurity.

events.title: DPRK Constitutionally Enshrines Nuclear Force as 'Irreversible'
events.detail: In September 2022, North Korea amended its constitution to enshrine its status as a nuclear-armed state, making it 'irreversible.' Kim Jong-un declared: 'The nuclear force will be updated and developed with the highest priority.' The law on nuclear weapons policy adopted September 8, 2022 outlined conditions for nuclear weapons use including 'imminent nuclear or WMD attack' and attacks on DPRK leadership. This effectively ended any prospect of denuclearization negotiations on prior terms.

sources.name: 38 North analysis of DPRK nuclear law

events.title: Hwasong-17 ICBM Test — Longest-Range DPRK Missile (63 Launches in 2022)
events.detail: DPRK fired 63 ballistic missiles in 2022 — a single-year record — capped by the Hwasong-17 ICBM test on November 18, 2022. The Hwasong-17, the largest DPRK missile ever tested, reached ~6,000 km altitude and is estimated to have a maximum range exceeding 15,000 km, capable of striking any US city. US and South Korea responded with air exercises including B-1B bomber deployments. The 2022 campaign signalled the end of Kim's self-imposed moratorium.

events.title: DPRK Launches First Military Reconnaissance Satellite
events.detail: On November 21, 2023, North Korea successfully launched its first military reconnaissance satellite, Malligyong-1, on a Chollima-1 rocket from Sohae — the third attempt after failures in May and August 2023. US Space Command confirmed orbital insertion. DPRK claimed the satellite immediately photographed US military bases. South Korea temporarily suspended part of the 2018 inter-Korean military agreement in response. Japan and the US condemned the launch as a violation of UN Security Council resolutions.

events.title: Putin Visits Pyongyang — Comprehensive Strategic Partnership Treaty
events.detail: Russian President Vladimir Putin visited Pyongyang on June 19, 2024 — his first visit in 24 years — and signed a 'Comprehensive Strategic Partnership Treaty' with Kim Jong-un that included a mutual defense clause. Putin stated the treaty committed both nations to provide military assistance if either faced armed aggression. The treaty marked the most significant upgrade of Russia-DPRK relations since the Soviet era. Western governments condemned the agreement.

media.source: Wikipedia / North Korean%E2%80%93Russian Treaty on Comprehensive Strategic Partnership

events.title: Kim Jong-un Ends Reunification Goal — South Korea Declared 'Hostile State'
events.detail: At the Supreme People's Assembly on January 15, 2024, Kim Jong-un declared South Korea a 'hostile foreign state' and announced that reunification was no longer a DPRK policy goal. The constitution was amended to remove all references to a unified Korea. In October 2024, the iconic Arch of Reunification — a 30-metre monument built by Kim Jong-il in 2001 — was demolished on Kim's orders. This represented the most explicit DPRK repudiation of Korean reunification in 70 years.

sources.name: South Korean Ministry of Unification response

events.title: DPRK Deploys ~12,000 Troops to Russia for Ukraine War
events.detail: South Korean National Intelligence Service confirmed in October 2024 that approximately 10,000–12,000 North Korean special operations soldiers were deployed to Russia's Kursk Oblast to fight alongside Russian forces against Ukraine. The US, UK, and NATO corroborated the deployment. Ukrainian forces confirmed contact with DPRK units and reported significant North Korean casualties. DPRK denied the deployment; Russia acknowledged 'military cooperation' without specific confirmation. US imposed additional sanctions on both countries.

media.source: Wikipedia / North Korean involvement in the Russo-Ukrainian war (2022%E2%80%93present)
